---
title: "Session 13: Advanced Quarto"
subtitle: "R Programming Workbook"
author: "R Programming Course"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Learning Objectives

::: {.callout-note title="Learning Objectives"}
By the end of this session you will be able to:

1. Use advanced chunk options like `results`, `layout-ncol`, and figure/table labels
2. Create cross-references for figures, tables, and sections
3. Use conditional content to show/hide material by output format or profile
4. Include external files and use shortcodes
5. Install and use Quarto extensions (shortcodes, filters, journal templates)
6. Create custom formats with SCSS theming
7. Batch render parameterized documents
8. Publish documents to Quarto Pub, GitHub Pages, and Netlify
9. Configure freeze for efficient website/blog rendering
:::

## Part 1: Advanced Chunk Options and Content Control

### Output Control with `results`

The `results` chunk option controls how R output is treated:

```{r}
#| results: markup

# Default behavior: output is formatted as code output
head(mtcars, 3)
```

Compare with `results: asis`, which treats output as raw markdown:

```{r}
#| results: asis

cat("**This text is bold** because R output is treated as markdown.\n\n")
cat("| Column A | Column B |\n")
cat("|----------|----------|\n")
cat("| 1        | 2        |\n")
cat("| 3        | 4        |\n")
```

The `asis` option is powerful for generating dynamic content. The output from `cat()` is interpreted as markdown rather than displayed as code output.

### Programmatic Content Generation

Use `results: asis` to build reports from data:

```{r}
#| results: asis

datasets <- list(
  mtcars = mtcars,
  iris = iris,
  airquality = airquality
)

for (name in names(datasets)) {
  cat(sprintf("### %s\n\n", name))
  cat(sprintf("- **Rows:** %d\n", nrow(datasets[[name]])))
  cat(sprintf("- **Columns:** %d\n", ncol(datasets[[name]])))
  cat(sprintf("- **Column names:** %s\n\n", paste(names(datasets[[name]]), collapse = ", ")))
}
```

Each iteration generates a markdown section with a heading and bullet points. This technique scales to any number of datasets, regions, or time periods.

### Your Turn: Dynamic Report Section

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Use `results: asis` to generate a summary section for each cylinder group in `mtcars`. For each group, output:

- A level-3 heading with the cylinder count
- The number of cars in that group
- The average MPG
- The average horsepower

```{r}
#| results: asis
#| code-fold: true

# Your code here:
for (cyl_val in sort(unique(mtcars$cyl))) {
  subset_data <- mtcars[mtcars$cyl == cyl_val, ]
  cat(sprintf("### %d Cylinders\n\n", cyl_val))
  cat(sprintf("- Cars: %d\n", nrow(subset_data)))
  cat(sprintf("- Avg MPG: %.1f\n", mean(subset_data$mpg)))
  cat(sprintf("- Avg HP: %.0f\n\n", mean(subset_data$hp)))
}
```
:::

### Figure Options and Cross-References

Label figures for cross-referencing:

```{r}
#| label: fig-mpg-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Relationship between car weight and fuel efficiency"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

plot(mtcars$wt, mtcars$mpg,
     pch = 19, col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
     ylab = "Miles per Gallon",
     main = "")
abline(lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars), col = "coral", lwd = 2)
```

You can reference this figure with `@fig-mpg-scatter` in your text. The label must start with `fig-` for figure cross-references to work.

### Table Cross-References

```{r}
#| label: tbl-cylinder-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Average statistics by cylinder count"

cyl_summary <- aggregate(
  cbind(mpg, hp, wt) ~ cyl,
  data = mtcars,
  FUN = mean
)
names(cyl_summary) <- c("Cylinders", "Avg MPG", "Avg HP", "Avg Weight")
knitr::kable(cyl_summary, digits = 1)
```

Table labels must start with `tbl-` and use `tbl-cap` for the caption.

### Multi-Plot Layouts

Place multiple plots in a grid:

```{r}
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| fig-cap:
#|   - "MPG distribution"
#|   - "Horsepower distribution"

hist(mtcars$mpg, main = "", col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "Miles per Gallon", breaks = 10)
hist(mtcars$hp, main = "", col = "coral",
     xlab = "Horsepower", breaks = 10)
```

The `layout-ncol: 2` option arranges plots in two columns. Each plot gets its own caption from the `fig-cap` list.

### Your Turn: Labeled Figures

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Create two labeled plots of the `iris` dataset:

1. A scatter plot of `Sepal.Length` vs `Petal.Length` with label `fig-iris-scatter` and a caption
2. A boxplot of `Sepal.Width` by `Species` with label `fig-iris-box` and a caption

Use `layout-ncol: 2` to display them side by side.

```{r}
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| label: fig-iris-plots
#| fig-cap:
#|   - "Sepal length vs petal length"
#|   - "Sepal width by species"

# Plot 1: scatter
plot(iris$Sepal.Length, iris$Petal.Length,
     pch = 19, col = as.numeric(iris$Species),
     xlab = "Sepal Length", ylab = "Petal Length")

# Plot 2: boxplot
boxplot(Sepal.Width ~ Species, data = iris,
        col = c("steelblue", "coral", "forestgreen"))
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Using the wrong label prefix for cross-references:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Wrong: label does not start with fig-
#| label: scatter-plot
#| fig-cap: "My plot"

# Result: @scatter-plot appears as plain text, not "Figure 1"

# Right: label starts with fig-
#| label: fig-scatter-plot
#| fig-cap: "My plot"

# Result: @fig-scatter-plot renders as "Figure 1" with a link
```

Prefix rules: `fig-` for figures, `tbl-` for tables, `eq-` for equations. No prefix, no cross-reference.
:::

### Conditional Content

Show different content based on output format:

````{verbatim}
::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
This interactive widget only appears in HTML:
```{r}
# plotly code here
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
Static version for PDF:
```{r}
# base R plot here
```
:::
````

### Conditional Content with Profiles

Create student and instructor versions from one source:

````{verbatim}
# In _quarto.yml:
profile:
  group:
    - [student, instructor]

# In your .qmd:
::: {.content-visible when-profile="instructor"}
**Answer:** The p-value is 0.023, indicating significance at alpha = 0.05.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="student"}
Calculate the p-value and interpret the result.
:::
````

Render with `quarto render --profile instructor` or `quarto render --profile student`.

### Your Turn: Conditional Document

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Write a short quiz document that shows questions for students and answers for instructors using profile-based conditional content. Include at least 3 questions about R.

Write the YAML configuration and the conditional content blocks:

```{r}
#| eval: false

quiz_yml <- '
profile:
  group:
    - [student, instructor]
'

quiz_content <- '
## Question 1
What does `length(c(1, 2, NA, 4))` return?

::: {.content-visible when-profile="student"}
Write your answer here: ___
:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="instructor"}
**Answer:** 4. `length()` counts all elements including NA.
:::

## Question 2
What is the difference between `[` and `[[` for lists?

::: {.content-visible when-profile="student"}
Write your answer here: ___
:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="instructor"}
**Answer:** `[` returns a sub-list, `[[` extracts a single element.
:::
'

cat(quiz_yml, "\n---\n", quiz_content)
```
:::

### Includes and Shortcodes

Pull in content from external files:

````{verbatim}
{{< include _methods.qmd >}}
````

The included file's content replaces the shortcode. Use underscore prefix (`_methods.qmd`) for partial files that are not standalone documents.

Built-in shortcodes:

| Shortcode | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `{{< include file.qmd >}}` | Insert file content |
| `{{< pagebreak >}}` | Page break (PDF) |
| `{{< kbd Ctrl+S >}}` | Keyboard shortcut styling |
| `{{< meta title >}}` | Insert YAML metadata value |
| `{{< video url >}}` | Embed video |

### Your Turn: Document with Includes

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Plan a research report that uses includes. Write:

1. The main document structure with `{{< include >}}` shortcodes
2. The content for each included file

```{r}
#| eval: false

main_doc <- '
---
title: "Research Report"
format: html
---

## Introduction

{{< include _intro.qmd >}}

## Methods

{{< include _methods.qmd >}}

## Results

{{< include _results.qmd >}}

## Discussion

{{< include _discussion.qmd >}}
'

intro_content <- '
This study examines the relationship between...
'

methods_content <- '
We collected data from...
'

cat(main_doc)
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge"}
Create a parameterized document that generates a report for any column in `mtcars`. The parameter should be the column name. The report should include:

- A histogram of the column
- Summary statistics
- A labeled, cross-referenceable figure

Write the YAML with params and the body using `params$column`:

```{r}
#| eval: false

param_report <- '
---
title: "Column Analysis"
params:
  column: "mpg"
format: html
---
'
cat(param_report)

# Then write code that uses params$column:
# hist(mtcars[[params$column]])
# summary(mtcars[[params$column]])
```
:::

## Part 2: Quarto Extensions and Custom Formats

### Installing Extensions

Extensions are Quarto plugins installed per-project:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# In your terminal (not R console):
# quarto add quarto-ext/fontawesome
# quarto add quarto-ext/lightbox
```

Extensions install into `_extensions/` in your project directory:

```
my-project/
  _extensions/
    quarto-ext/
      fontawesome/
        _extension.yml
        ...
  document.qmd
```

### Shortcode Extensions

After installing `fontawesome`:

````{verbatim}
{{< fa brands github >}} View on GitHub
{{< fa solid chart-line >}} Analysis
{{< fa regular envelope >}} Email
````

The syntax is `fa [style] [icon-name]`. Styles: `solid`, `regular`, `brands`.

### Filter Extensions

Filters transform your document during rendering:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Install latex-environment filter:
# quarto add quarto-ext/latex-environment
```

````{verbatim}
---
filters:
  - latex-environment
environments: [theorem, proof]
---

::: {.theorem}
For all n >= 1, the sum of integers from 1 to n equals n(n+1)/2.
:::
````

### Journal Article Templates

Write papers formatted for specific journals:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Install Journal of Statistical Software template:
# quarto add quarto-journals/jss
```

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My Paper"
format:
  jss-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
  jss-html: default
---
````

Available journals: JSS, PLOS, Elsevier, ACM, ASA, and more.

### Your Turn: Extension Research

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Browse the Quarto extensions listing at <https://quarto.org/docs/extensions/> and identify:

1. Two shortcode extensions you would use in a blog
2. One filter extension useful for academic writing
3. One journal template relevant to your field

Write your findings:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Extension 1 (shortcode):
# Name:
# Purpose:
# Install command:

# Extension 2 (shortcode):
# Name:
# Purpose:
# Install command:

# Extension 3 (filter):
# Name:
# Purpose:
# Install command:

# Extension 4 (journal):
# Name:
# Purpose:
# Install command:
```
:::

### Custom Formats with SCSS

Customize any Quarto theme with SCSS:

```{r}
#| eval: false

custom_scss <- '
/*-- scss:defaults --*/
$body-bg: #fafafa;
$body-color: #2c3e50;
$link-color: #2980b9;
$font-size-base: 1.05rem;
$code-bg: #ecf0f1;
$code-color: #c0392b;

/*-- scss:rules --*/
h1, h2, h3 {
  color: #2c3e50;
  font-weight: 600;
  border-bottom: 2px solid #3498db;
  padding-bottom: 0.3em;
}

.callout-note {
  border-left-color: #2980b9;
}

.callout-warning {
  border-left-color: #e67e22;
}
'
cat(custom_scss)
```

Reference in YAML:

````{verbatim}
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, custom.scss]
````

The array syntax applies the base theme first, then your overrides.

### Your Turn: Custom Theme

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Write an SCSS file that creates a dark academic theme:

- Dark background (#1a1a2e)
- Light text (#e0e0e0)
- Gold accents for headings (#f39c12)
- Monospace code with green text (#2ecc71) on dark background (#0d0d1a)

```{r}
#| eval: false

dark_theme <- '
/*-- scss:defaults --*/
# Your variables here

/*-- scss:rules --*/
# Your custom rules here
'
cat(dark_theme)
```
:::

### Batch Rendering Parameterized Documents

Generate multiple reports from one template:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Template: report-template.qmd has params$cyl
cyl_values <- c(4, 6, 8)

for (cyl in cyl_values) {
  quarto::quarto_render(
    input = "report-template.qmd",
    output_file = paste0("report-", cyl, "-cyl.html"),
    execute_params = list(cyl = cyl)
  )
}
# Produces: report-4-cyl.html, report-6-cyl.html, report-8-cyl.html
```

The template uses `params$cyl` to filter data and generate customized content. One source file, multiple outputs.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Forgetting to use `params$variable` inside the template:

Wrong:
```{r}
#| eval: false
# Hardcoded value in the template:
filtered <- mtcars[mtcars$cyl == 4, ]
```

Right:
```{r}
#| eval: false
# Using the parameter:
filtered <- mtcars[mtcars$cyl == params$cyl, ]
```

If you hardcode values, every rendered version will show the same data regardless of parameters.
:::

### Your Turn: Batch Rendering Plan

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Design a parameterized report template for the `iris` dataset. The parameter should be `species`. Write:

1. The YAML front matter with the parameter
2. The R code that filters by `params$species`
3. The loop that renders one report per species

```{r}
#| eval: false

# 1. YAML:
yaml_header <- '
---
title: "Iris Species Report"
params:
  species: "setosa"
format: html
---
'

# 2. Template code:
template_code <- '
filtered <- iris[iris$Species == params$species, ]
summary(filtered)
plot(filtered$Sepal.Length, filtered$Petal.Length)
'

# 3. Rendering loop:
species_list <- c("setosa", "versicolor", "virginica")
for (sp in species_list) {
  # quarto::quarto_render(
  #   "iris-report.qmd",
  #   output_file = paste0("iris-", sp, ".html"),
  #   execute_params = list(species = sp)
  # )
}
cat(yaml_header)
```
:::

## Part 3: Publishing Workflows

### Publishing Options

| Platform | Cost | Setup | Best For |
|----------|------|-------|----------|
| Quarto Pub | Free | Minimal | Quick sharing, class projects |
| GitHub Pages | Free | Moderate | Portfolios, open source |
| Netlify | Free tier | Moderate | Professional sites |
| Posit Connect | Paid | Complex | Enterprise, authentication |

### Publishing to Quarto Pub

The fastest way to share your work:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# In terminal:
# quarto publish quarto-pub

# First time: prompts for account creation
# Subsequent: updates existing publication

# Result: https://username.quarto.pub/document-name/
```

Quarto Pub is public-only with 100 MB storage. Good enough for most student projects.

### Publishing to GitHub Pages

Two approaches:

**Approach 1: Render locally, push `docs/`**

````{verbatim}
# _quarto.yml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
````

Push to GitHub, enable Pages from `docs/` in repo settings.

**Approach 2: GitHub Actions automation**

````{verbatim}
# .github/workflows/publish.yml
on:
  push:
    branches: main

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2
      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/render@v2
      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/publish@v2
        with:
          target: gh-pages
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
````

### Your Turn: Publishing Plan

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Choose a publishing platform for your course portfolio. Write:

1. Your chosen platform and why
2. The `_quarto.yml` configuration for your site
3. The steps to publish (in order)

```{r}
#| eval: false

# 1. Platform choice:
# I chose: ___
# Because: ___

# 2. _quarto.yml:
config <- '
project:
  type: website
  # your config
'

# 3. Publishing steps:
steps <- c(
  "Step 1: ...",
  "Step 2: ...",
  "Step 3: ..."
)
cat(paste(steps, collapse = "\n"))
```
:::

### Freeze for Performance

For websites and blogs, `freeze` controls when code re-executes:

````{verbatim}
# _quarto.yml
execute:
  freeze: auto    # re-execute only when source changes
````

- `freeze: true` — never re-execute
- `freeze: auto` — re-execute only when source `.qmd` changes
- `freeze: false` — always re-execute (default)

Cached results live in `_freeze/`. Always commit this directory to Git.

### Your Turn: Blog Configuration

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Write the complete `_quarto.yml` for a data science blog with:

- Website type with navbar (Blog, About, GitHub link)
- Flatly/darkly theme toggle
- Freeze set to auto
- RSS feed enabled on the listing page
- Code folding enabled

```{r}
#| eval: false

blog_config <- '
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site

website:
  title: "Your Blog Name"
  # Complete the configuration

format:
  html:
    # Complete the format options

execute:
  # Complete execution options
'
cat(blog_config)
```
:::

### SEO and Social Sharing

Help search engines and social media display your content:

````{verbatim}
---
title: "My Analysis"
description: "A short summary for search engines and social cards"
author:
  - name: "Your Name"
    url: https://yoursite.com
date: "2024-03-15"
categories: [R, analysis]
image: "thumbnail.png"
open-graph: true
twitter-card: true
---
````

`open-graph` and `twitter-card` generate metadata for social media preview cards. `description` and `image` control what appears in those cards.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Forgetting to commit `_freeze/` to Git:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# If _freeze/ is in .gitignore:
# - GitHub Actions has no cached results
# - Every page re-executes on every build
# - Builds are slow and may fail

# Solution: make sure _freeze/ is NOT in .gitignore
# Commit it alongside your source files
```
:::

### Your Turn: Complete Publishing Workflow

::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn"}
Write out the complete sequence of commands to:

1. Create a new Quarto blog project
2. Write a blog post with R code and a ggplot
3. Preview locally
4. Initialize a Git repository
5. Push to GitHub
6. Publish to GitHub Pages

```{r}
#| eval: false

workflow <- '
# Step 1: Create project
# quarto create project website my-blog

# Step 2: Write post
# mkdir posts/2024-first-post
# Create posts/2024-first-post/index.qmd with your content

# Step 3: Preview
# quarto preview

# Step 4: Git setup
# git init
# git add -A
# git commit -m "Initial blog"

# Step 5: Push to GitHub
# git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-blog.git
# git push -u origin main

# Step 6: Publish
# quarto publish gh-pages
'
cat(workflow)
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge"}
Design a complete course website that includes:

1. A home page with course overview
2. A sidebar with links to each session (Sessions 1-15)
3. Each session page has slides (embedded or linked) and workbook content
4. An about page with instructor info
5. A blog section for announcements
6. Freeze enabled for performance
7. Published to GitHub Pages

Write the `_quarto.yml`, directory structure, and at least one session page.
:::

## Session Synthesis

### Connect the Dots

::: {.callout-important title="Synthesis Exercise"}
Build a complete publishing workflow for a data analysis project:

1. Create a parameterized report template that analyzes one variable from `mtcars`
2. Use `results: asis` to programmatically generate section headings
3. Include labeled, cross-referenceable figures and tables
4. Write the SCSS for a custom theme
5. Write the batch rendering loop for all numeric columns
6. Write the `_quarto.yml` to publish as a website with GitHub Pages
7. Include freeze configuration

Deliver all components:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# 1. Report template YAML:
template_yaml <- '
---
title: "mtcars Variable Report"
params:
  variable: "mpg"
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, custom.scss]
    toc: true
execute:
  freeze: auto
---
'

# 2. Template body with results: asis, labeled figures, tables
# 3. custom.scss content
# 4. Batch rendering loop
# 5. _quarto.yml for website
# 6. GitHub Actions workflow

cat(template_yaml)
```
:::

### Key Takeaways

1. `results: asis` turns R output into raw markdown for dynamic content generation
2. Cross-references need correct prefixes: `fig-` for figures, `tbl-` for tables
3. Conditional content with profiles creates student/instructor versions from one source
4. Extensions install per-project and add shortcodes, filters, or journal formats
5. `freeze: auto` caches computation results so blogs render fast
6. Commit `_freeze/` to version control or builds will re-execute everything

### Prepare for Next Session

Next session covers professional practices:

- Project organization with `here` package and `renv`
- Debugging and profiling in Positron
- Code style with tidyverse style guide, `lintr`, and `styler`

Before next session:

- Publish at least one document to Quarto Pub or GitHub Pages
- Install the `here` and `renv` packages: `install.packages(c("here", "renv"))`
- Read the tidyverse style guide at <https://style.tidyverse.org/>
